channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Clive Barker’s Hellraiser: Revival Is A Wild Trip Of Brutal Combat And Raunchy Horror
item.content:encoded:
Horror as a genre has become larger and larger in the Video Game space, from indie titles to large franchises releasing explosive sequels. At Summer Game Fest this year, we had the chance to sit down and play through a portion of Clive Barker’s Hellraiser: Revival.

In Clive Barker’s Hellraiser: Revival you play as Aiden, a man who, after opening the puzzle box known as the Genesis Configuration while taking part in some very gore-oriented kink-play, had to watch as his girlfriend was taken/killed by the Cenobites that were summoned.

As the name implies, Clive Barker, writer of the Hellraiser books, is a core member of the creative team for Revival. In addition to that, Doug Bradley, the actor who originally brought the Hell Priest Cenobite to life, is reprising his role, voicing the character.

My gameplay demo jumped around to a few different parts of the story to make sure that I was able to get a good taste of everything that the final game will have to offer. For the first segment of the game, which I was told takes place a few hours into the game, Aiden found his way into a BDSM club and needed to progress through it for a reason that was unknown to me.

Cultists In A Sex Club

The set decoration in this location was phenomenal. An entrance hall with vaulted ceilings and flaming braziers decked out in shining golds and velvety reds screamed ‘sex club’. Stepping into the side rooms, you were greeted with changing rooms decked out in just about anything leather or with straps that you could put on, around, or in someone.

Moving through this space, it was clear that Aiden was an intruder, as the workers at this bar were quick to escalate and attempt to kill you. At this point, Aiden’s arsenal included a variety of weapons: a machete, a pistol, and a shotgun (there was also an SMG that could be picked up in a back room), but also the power of the Cube.

Dropped metal objects could be summoned with the cube and fired off at will. You could also draw the blazing fire towards you, drawing it into the cube to then shoot at an enemy. These environmental uses of the puzzle box came up quite frequently in a small space, but once you had summoned an item to yourself, it would only stay for a short amount of time before dropping or flaring out. I imagine there are going to be players who really get into a flow state, combining regular ranged attacks, melee attacks, and the environment to decimate some opponents.

Once things really kicked off, and a variety of partially armored enemies jumped out of Iron Maidens, the combat really kicked it up a notch, as did the difficulty. Aiden is not a character who is able to handle much. It wasn’t long at all before I was staring at a game-over screen.

Starting again, and with the knowledge that you really need to keep your distance, I was able to defeat each of the enemies in the room. Many were wearing metal masks, assumedly because they got off on it, and so during my first attempt at the room, I tried to aim at their exposed flesh. On the repeat attempt, I tried to shoot the metal directly and found that I could shoot it off in a very satisfying manner.

I think there was still some work to do on the ragdoll animations of the deceased enemies falling. With each one I killed, their limbs would all extend at once, causing them to almost spring like a frog for a moment before collapsing to the ground. After the tense combat, it was a much-appreciated moment of levity, though.

While I did have a melee option, I didn’t end up using it, instead favoring the guns in my arsenal. I felt the melee was a bit too difficult to risk getting in close; a shotgun blast from an enemy would very quickly put me on my ass, and I didn’t want to be a sitting duck for another of these crazed cultists to come up and grab me.

While I was only just getting used to the controls, there were some nice quality of life mechanics to keep your head on a swivel. There is a jump back button that lets you very quickly try to make some distance between you and a would-be attacker, on top of that, there was a quick pivot key too, letting you immediately rotate 180 degrees.

After making my way through the sex club, I entered into a puzzle-oriented part of the demo.

Puzzles of geometry and P.T.-esc hellscapes

I was immediately dropped into a misty stone corridor, the signature Hellraised eerie blue light shining through the gaps towards me. With the only way to move ahead of me, I continued turning left and right in what felt like a maze, even though it was a linear experience.

After a few turns, the hallway expanded out into a large chamber where I had to get from my side of the room to the other across a series of narrow stone platforms. This room was a barrel broken up into three segments that I was on the inside of. I didn’t just have to make my way across, but I also had to rotate the three sections to ensure that the path I was hoping to take would line up to get me to freedom. Taking the wrong path would lead me to a dead end, and that’s before we even talk about the spinning blades coming out of the walls at odd intervals.

The final portion of the demo was still puzzle-oriented, but leaned heavily into the psychological horror of titles like P.T or Layers of Fear. As Aiden, I was stuck in a loop of a hallway, in what I can only assume is Aiden’s house. I had the ability to go down two hallways, up the stairs, and through a door before I’d return to the start of the first hallway.

You needed to pick up on key differences, follow the prompts in the vocal cues, and interact with objects that prompted Aiden to talk about the relationship with his girlfriend, and reveal the kinds of fantasies he had about her.

There is a LOT of sex, nudity, and hardcore kink play here, so this game will definitely not be for the squeamish.

While the experience I had was slightly jarring between tough, fast-paced combat, eerie logic puzzles, and horror-inspired puzzling it was a good encapsulation of everything that this game will have to offer when it releases.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/clive-barkers-hellraiser-revival-is-wild-trip-of-brutal-combat-and-raunchy-horror
